phase.title: Registry Exploitation
phase.summary: Registry-based privilege escalation: AlwaysInstallElevated, AutoLogon credentials, AutoRun keys, modifiable hijack keys.

nodes.title: AlwaysInstallElevated MSI as SYSTEM
nodes.summary: If both HKLM and HKCU AlwaysInstallElevated == 1, any MSI runs as SYSTEM.
nodes.description_md: Cross-linked from `svc-always-installed-elevated` in Phase 5 because it's often discovered via service/registry enum.

snippets.title: Probe both hives
snippets.code:
reg query HKLM\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated
reg query HKCU\Software\Policies\Microsoft\Windows\Installer /v AlwaysInstallElevated

nodes.title: AutoLogon credentials in Winlogon
nodes.summary: DefaultUserName / DefaultPassword in plaintext under HKLM\...\Winlogon when AutoAdminLogon is enabled.

snippets.title: reg query Winlogon
snippets.code: reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon" /v DefaultDomainName /v DefaultUserName /v DefaultPassword /v AutoAdminLogon

snippets.title: PowerShell read
snippets.code: Get-ItemProperty 'HKLM:\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon' | Select AutoAdminLogon, DefaultUserName, DefaultPassword

nodes.description_md:
Windows AutoLogon stores credentials in plaintext under `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon` when an administrator configures automatic user login (e.g., kiosk, headless server). The `DefaultUserName` and `DefaultPassword` values are readable by any user without requiring administrator rights.

**What to look for**:
- `AutoAdminLogon = 1` — confirms autologon is configured
- `DefaultUserName` — the username (may include domain prefix)
- `DefaultPassword` — plaintext password
- `DefaultDomainName` — optional domain

**Impact**: The recovered credentials are often reused across the environment. Test them via `runas`, SMB (`smbclient`), or RDP before moving on.

**Prevalence**: Very common in industrial, retail, and education environments that configure kiosk-style workstations.
nodes.detection_hints: Registry reads of `DefaultPassword` by non-SYSTEM accounts (EID 4663 if object auditing is configured); the credential is a static secret — rotate immediately on finding.

nodes.title: AutoRun keys with writable targets
nodes.summary: Run / RunOnce keys can reference binaries in writable directories — replace and wait for next admin logon.

snippets.title: Dump autorun keys
snippets.code:
reg query HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run
reg query HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce
reg query HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run

nodes.description_md:
AutoRun and AutoStart registry keys execute binaries at user logon or system startup. The key security check is whether the **target binary** (not the key itself) is stored in a writable directory. If a SYSTEM user or an admin has a run-key pointing to `C:\Tools\updater.exe`, and `C:\Tools\` is world-writable, you can overwrite the binary.

**Keys to check**:
- `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` (all users)
- `HKLM\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce`
- `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\Run` (current user)
- `HKCU\Software\Microsoft\Windows\CurrentVersion\RunOnce`

**Workflow**: Dump key values, extract binary paths, run `icacls` or `accesschk` against each. A writable target binary + admin/SYSTEM runs it = SYSTEM execution on next logon.
nodes.detection_hints: Writes to Run/RunOnce key values (EID 13 in Sysmon); file writes to directories referenced by existing autorun entries; EID 4688 for unexpected processes launched at logon.

nodes.title: Saved PuTTY sessions (proxy passwords)
nodes.summary: PuTTY stores proxy credentials in cleartext under HKCU.
nodes.description_md:
PuTTY stores saved SSH session configurations under `HKCU\Software\SimonTatham\PuTTY\Sessions\`. Each named session may contain proxy credentials in cleartext: `ProxyUsername` and `ProxyPassword` values under the session key.

**What's recoverable**:
- `ProxyUsername` / `ProxyPassword` — proxy auth credentials (cleartext)
- `HostName` / `PortNumber` — target host information
- `UserName` — SSH username (but not the key or SSH password)

**Scope**: These credentials are scoped to the current user's HKCU hive. If you can read another user's hive (via SeBackupPrivilege or from a shadow copy), check their sessions too.

**Exploitation**: Proxy credentials often reuse corporate passwords. Test recovered credentials against VPN, web proxies, and other SSH targets.
nodes.detection_hints: Registry reads of PuTTY session keys; enumeration of all user hives by non-SYSTEM accounts.

nodes.title: Modifiable Winlogon\Shell value
nodes.summary: When the Shell value is writable, replace it; next interactive logon spawns your payload as the logging-in user.
nodes.description_md:
The `Shell` value under `HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Winlogon` specifies the user-shell process launched by Winlogon at interactive logon. Normally set to `explorer.exe`, it can be overwritten (if the key is writable) to any binary.

**Attack scenario**:
1. Verify `Shell` key is writable: `accesschk.exe /accepteula -kvuqsw hklm\...\Winlogon`
2. Write your payload: `reg add "HKLM\...\Winlogon" /v Shell /d "C:\Temp\payload.exe" /f`
3. Wait for the next interactive logon — the logging-in user's context executes your payload

**Persistence vs. escalation**: If the victim is a standard user, you gain their context. If an admin logs in next, you gain admin. If SYSTEM auto-logs in, you get SYSTEM.

**Note**: Breaking `Shell` stops Explorer from launching — the user sees a blank desktop. Use a wrapper that spawns the original `explorer.exe,C:\Temp\payload.exe` to avoid detection.
nodes.detection_hints: Modifications to the Winlogon `Shell` registry value (Sysmon EID 13); unexpected processes as the immediate child of `winlogon.exe` at logon; blank desktop after login reported by users.

nodes.title: Image File Execution Options (IFEO) Debugger hijack
nodes.summary: Set a Debugger value under IFEO to launch your binary whenever the target executable starts.

snippets.title: Sticky-keys IFEO trick
snippets.code: reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\sethc.exe" /v Debugger /t REG_SZ /d "C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe" /f

nodes.detection_hints: Any IFEO Debugger value pointing at an interactive shell is highly suspicious.
nodes.description_md:
The Image File Execution Options (IFEO) registry key (`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Image File Execution Options\`) is used by Windows for debugging — a `Debugger` value causes Windows to launch the specified debugger whenever the target executable is started, passing the original process as an argument.

An attacker with admin rights can set `Debugger=C:\Windows\System32\cmd.exe` for any binary, causing cmd to launch whenever that program runs.

**Accessibility tool hijack (classic technique)**:
- Target: `sethc.exe` (Sticky Keys), `utilman.exe` (Ease of Access), `osk.exe` (On-Screen Keyboard)
- These binaries launch at the Windows login screen as SYSTEM
- Setting `Debugger=cmd.exe` on them spawns a SYSTEM command prompt at the lock screen

**Post-admin use**: This technique requires write to `HKLM` (admin), so it's typically used for **persistence** or to exploit a locked machine, not initial escalation.

nodes.title: AppInit_DLLs injection
nodes.summary: DLLs in AppInit_DLLs load into every user32-linked process — admin write = broad code exec.
nodes.description_md: If `LoadAppInit_DLLs` is enabled and you can write `AppInit_DLLs`, your DLL loads into nearly every GUI process. Disabled by default under Secure Boot, but still seen on legacy/server builds.
nodes.detection_hints: Writes to HKLM\...\Windows\AppInit_DLLs and unexpected modules loaded across many processes.

snippets.title: Check & set
snippets.code:
reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows" /v AppInit_DLLs
reg add "HKLM\SOFTWARE\Microsoft\Windows NT\CurrentVersion\Windows" /v AppInit_DLLs /d "C:\Temp\evil.dll" /f

nodes.title: Run / RunOnce key write
nodes.summary: Writable HKLM Run keys execute your payload as the next user to log on.
nodes.description_md: `HKLM\...\Run` and `RunOnce` execute at logon. If a higher-privileged user logs in after you plant a value, your code runs in their context. Check ACLs on the keys.

nodes.title: Winlogon Userinit / Shell append
nodes.summary: Append your binary to Userinit/Shell so it runs at every interactive logon.
nodes.description_md: `Userinit` and `Shell` under the Winlogon key are comma-separated lists executed at logon. Appending a path (keeping `userinit.exe,`/`explorer.exe,`) runs your code for the logging-on user.

nodes.title: Service ImagePath via registry
nodes.summary: Writable service key lets you rewrite ImagePath to your binary.
nodes.description_md: Service configuration lives under `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\<svc>`. If the key ACL grants you write, change `ImagePath` and restart the service to run as its (often SYSTEM) account — the registry twin of `sc config binPath`.

snippets.title: Rewrite ImagePath
snippets.code:
reg add "HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\VulnSvc" /v ImagePath /t REG_EXPAND_SZ /d "C:\Temp\payload.exe" /f
sc start VulnSvc

nodes.title: System PATH manipulation
nodes.summary: Writable system PATH entry enables DLL/EXE hijacking against SYSTEM processes.
nodes.description_md: If the machine `PATH` (HKLM Environment) contains a directory you can write to, binaries/DLLs resolved by name from privileged processes can be hijacked. Pair with phantom-DLL hunting.

nodes.title: Stored VNC passwords
nodes.summary: RealVNC/TightVNC/UltraVNC store obfuscated passwords in the registry.
nodes.description_md: VNC servers persist a fixed-key DES-obfuscated password under their registry keys. Recover the blob and decrypt with the well-known key (e.g. `vncpwd`).

snippets.title: Common VNC keys
snippets.code:
reg query "HKLM\SOFTWARE\RealVNC\vncserver" /v Password
reg query "HKCU\Software\TightVNC\Server" /v Password

nodes.title: SNMP community strings
nodes.summary: SNMP config in the registry can leak community strings and credentials.
nodes.description_md: The SNMP service stores community strings under `HKLM\SYSTEM\CurrentControlSet\Services\SNMP\Parameters`. Read-write communities can pivot to device/router control.

nodes.title: Disable UAC via registry
nodes.summary: With admin, flip EnableLUA / ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin to remove the prompt.
nodes.description_md: Once at High integrity you can lower UAC for future logons by setting `EnableLUA=0` or `ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin=0`. Loud and persistent — better suited to lab work than stealthy ops.
nodes.detection_hints: Changes to EnableLUA / ConsentPromptBehaviorAdmin under Policies\System.
